name: Nasr
description: Nasr, meaning 'vulture' or 'eagle' in Arabic, was a pre-Islamic Arabian deity worshipped by the tribe of Himyar, particularly in the region of Yemen. He was one of the five idols mentioned in the Quran (Surah Nuh, 71:23) that the people of Noah worshipped, leading to their downfall according to Islamic tradition. Nasr was considered a male deity and was often associated with strength, power, and protection, fitting the image of a soaring bird of prey. His worship was prevalent in the southern Arabian Peninsula, and it is believed that images or representations of Nasr were kept in tribal shrines or temples. The veneration of Nasr, along with the other idols mentioned in the Quran, is seen as a form of idolatry and deviation from the true monotheistic path. The Quranic narrative emphasizes the abandonment of the worship of these idols as a crucial step towards embracing the worship of Allah. The specific rituals and practices associated with Nasr's worship are not extensively documented, but it is likely that they involved offerings, prayers, and perhaps even animal sacrifices, common practices in pre-Islamic Arabian religious traditions. The suppression of Nasr's worship occurred with the rise of Islam in the 7th century CE, as the new faith actively sought to dismantle the existing polytheistic systems and establish monotheism as the dominant religion of the Arabian Peninsula. Nasr's legacy remains primarily through his mention in the Quran as an example of the idols that led people astray.
subtitle: The Vulture Idol of Pre-Islamic Arabia

keyMyths.title: Mention in the Quran
keyMyths.description: Nasr is mentioned in the Quran (Surah Nuh) as one of the idols worshipped by the people of Noah. This is presented as a reason for their divine punishment.

keyMyths.title: Worship by the Himyar
keyMyths.description: Nasr was a prominent deity worshipped by the Himyar tribe in pre-Islamic Yemen. His veneration was linked to tribal identity and power.

keyMyths.title: Abandonment with Islam
keyMyths.description: With the rise of Islam, the worship of Nasr and other idols was suppressed. This marked a significant shift towards monotheism in the Arabian Peninsula.

sources.author: Ibn Ishaq
sources.description: Early biography of Muhammad, mentions pre-Islamic religious practices.
sources.text: Early biography of Muhammad, mentions pre-Islamic religious practices.
